1:
"And they came over unto the other side of the sea, into the country of the Gadarenes." The witnesses note the setting of the great deliverance and the textual question of the place-name. The grammatical-historical voices weigh the variants. Robertson: "The Gerasenes... Like Luke 8:26 while Matthew 8:28 has the Gadarenes. The ruins of the village Khersa (Gerasa) probably point to this site." Lightfoot harmonizes: "the region of the Gergesenes was of broader extent... and the region of the Gadarenes was included within it," Gadara being "a city of heathens (hence it is less marvel if there were swine among them)." Clarke notes Lightfoot's view that, of the two demoniacs, "one was of Gadara, and consequently a heathen, the other was a Gergesenian."

The Latin and Antiochene fathers read the whole narrative theologically. Theophylact: "now it is made known Who He is by the testimony of His enemies. For the demoniac came up confessing that He was the Son of God." Bede marvels at "how great is the impiety of the Jews, to say that He cast out devils by the prince of the devils, when the very devils confess that they have nothing in common with Him." Remigius explains the demons' request: "they cannot hurt men without Divine permission," and chose the swine "because no animal is more unclean than a hog, and devils always delight in filthiness." Bede reads the demoniac mystically as "the people of the Gentiles," who "expelled the enemy from the heart," and the swine drowned as those "freed from the empire of demons." Gregory draws the moral of the man's commission: "the Lord commands that the mind should first toil hard at its work, and afterwards should refresh itself with contemplation."

The puritan Henry gives the running exposition: Christ "came from heaven to earth, and returned, in a storm, to redeem a remnant of mankind out of the hands of the devil." The man's misery — dwelling among tombs, unbindable, self-cutting — "sets forth the sad condition of those souls in which the devil has dominion." The legion's confession of Christ shows "it is no strange thing to hear the best words drop from the worst mouths." The Gadarenes, finding their swine lost, "wished to have rather his room than his company," choosing "rather to abandon their Saviour" than part with their sins — "such strange misconstructions do carnal hearts make of the just judgments of God." The witnesses agree: in a heathen land beyond the sea, Christ confronts and conquers the most extreme case of demonic possession, displaying his absolute authority over the whole kingdom of Satan.
2:
"And when he was come out of the ship, immediately there met him out of the tombs a man with an unclean spirit." The witnesses note Mark's vivid immediacy and the difference in the number of demoniacs among the Gospels. Robertson: "Straightway Mark says, using the genitive absolute... The demoniac greeted Jesus at once. Mark and Luke 8:27 mention only one man while Matthew notes two demoniacs, perhaps one more violent than the other." Each Gospel has a different phrase: "Mark has a man with an unclean spirit, Matthew 8:28 two possessed with demons, Luke 8:27 one having demons. Mark has many touches about this miracle not retained in Matthew and Luke."

On the apparent discrepancy in number, the witnesses follow Augustine (under v.1): "Though Matthew says that there were two, Mark and Luke mention one, that you may understand that one of them was a more illustrious person, concerning whose state that country was much afflicted." Henry (under v.1) agreed: "If there were two, there was one, and Mark doth not say that there was but one; so that this difference cannot give us any just offence; it is probable that one of them was much more remarkable than the other, and said what was said." Wesley: "Probably this, so particularly spoken of here, was the most remarkably fierce and ungovernable."

Ellicott notes Mark's characteristic phrase: "A man with an unclean spirit... though not peculiar to St. Mark, is often used by him where the other Gospels have possessed with demons." The detail that the man came "out of the tombs" sets the scene of his wretched dwelling among the dead — a place of uncleanness and desolation, fitting habitation for the unclean spirit.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which introduces the demoniac as he rushes from the tombs to meet Christ at the moment of landing. The witnesses agree: the immediacy of the encounter — the demoniac confronting Jesus "straightway" as he steps from the boat — and the focus (in Mark and Luke) on the one more violent sufferer, set the stage for the great deliverance. The difference in number between Matthew and Mark is no contradiction but a matter of emphasis: Mark, drawing on Peter's eyewitness memory, fixes attention on the most remarkable of the two, whose dreadful condition (vv.3-5) and forced confession (v.7) display both the depth of Satan's tyranny and the supremacy of Christ over it. The man comes from among the dead — a living picture of the death-in-life that demonic bondage is.
3:
"Who had his dwelling among the tombs; and no man could bind him, no, not with chains." The witnesses note the man's wretched dwelling and his supernatural, unbindable strength. Gill observes that dwelling among the tombs "is one of the characters of a madman among the Jews." Ellicott marks the better reading: "no man could any longer bind him. The attempt had been so often made and baffled that it had been given up in despair." Robertson: "this demoniac snapped a handcuff as if a string."

The Reformed Calvin draws the doctrine from the man's preternatural strength: "Naturally, he was not able to break the chains; and hence we infer that Satan is sometimes permitted to make extraordinary movements, the effect of which goes beyond our comprehension and beyond ordinary means." Though "we often perceive in madmen much greater strength than belongs to their natural capacity," the force the Evangelists describe "was far greater." Calvin draws the lesson: "It was indeed a sad and shocking exhibition, but may serve to remind us how wretched and alarming it is to be placed under the tyranny of Satan, and also that bodily agony, however violent or cruel, is not more to be dreaded than distress of mind."

The dwelling among the tombs the witnesses (with Henry, under v.1) read as the devil's design: "Perhaps the devil drove him to the tombs, to make people fancy that the souls of the dead were turned into daemons"; and "the unclean spirit drives people into that company that is defiling, and so keeps possession of them." The tombs, ceremonially unclean (Numbers 19:16), were the fit haunt of the unclean spirit.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which begins the description of the demoniac's misery (vv.3-5). The witnesses agree: the man's dwelling among the dead and his unbindable strength display the dreadful reality and power of demonic possession. No human means — not chains, not fetters — could restrain him, for a power beyond nature was at work in him. Calvin's reflection gives the abiding lesson: the spectacle of this tyranny should remind us how wretched it is to be under Satan's dominion, and that the agony of a soul enslaved to the powers of darkness is more to be dreaded than any bodily torment. The man among the tombs is a living parable of the soul in bondage to sin, which no law or chain of human discipline can bind, and which only Christ can set free.
4:
"Because that he had been often bound with fetters and chains, and the chains had been plucked asunder by him, and the fetters broken in pieces: neither could any man tame him." The witnesses dwell on the futile attempts to restrain the demoniac and the supernatural strength that broke every bond. Gill: "Trial had been made several times, to no purpose; his arms had been bound with chains, and his feet with fetters." Robertson parses the perfect tenses describing the completed, repeated failures: "Often bound... with fetters and chains, bound hand and foot, but all to no purpose. Rent asunder — drawn in two... Broken in pieces — rubbed together, crushed together. Perhaps the neighbours who told the story could point to broken fragments of chains and fetters." And: "No man had strength to tame him. He roamed at will like a lion in the jungle."

Clarke draws the lesson against those who deny demonic agency: "His strength, it appears, was supernatural, no kind of chains being strong enough to confine him. With several, this man would have passed for an outrageous madman, and diabolic influence be entirely left out of the question; but it is the prerogative of the inspired penman only, to enter into the nature and causes of things." He confronts the skeptic: "Men sent from God, to bear witness to the truth, tell us there were demoniacs in their time; you say, No, they were only diseases. Whom shall we credit?"

Ellicott suggests the bonds "were not necessarily of metal," the snapping and wearing-away rather suggesting "ropes, or cords, as in the case of Samson" (Judges 15:13), and notes "the vivid fulness of the whole description is eminently characteristic of St. Mark's style."

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which completes the picture of the man's ungovernable strength. The witnesses agree: every human attempt to bind and tame the demoniac had failed, the chains plucked asunder and the fetters crushed, so that none could master him. The vivid detail — drawn, Robertson suggests, from eyewitnesses who could point to the broken fragments — both authenticates the account and magnifies the deliverance to come. Against the rationalists who would reduce the case to mere madness, the witnesses (with Clarke) insist on the reality of the diabolic agency the inspired writers plainly affirm. The man whom no chain could bind would, at Christ's word, be found "sitting, clothed, and in his right mind" (v.15) — the strength that defied all human bonds yielding instantly to the Lord of all.
5:
"And always, night and day, he was in the mountains, and in the tombs, crying, and cutting himself with stones." The witnesses note the ceaseless torment of the demoniac, a terror and torment to himself. Robertson catches Mark's further vivid details: "Night and day his loud scream or screech could be heard like other demoniacs... The verb for cutting himself occurs here only in the N.T... We say gash, hack to pieces. Perhaps he was scarred all over with such gashes during his moments of wild frenzy night and day in the tombs and on the mountains." Ellicott notes "this feature, again, is given only by St. Mark."

Clarke draws the lesson on Satan's cruelty: "In this person's case we see a specimen of what Satan could do in all the wicked, if God should permit him; but even the devil himself has his chain; and he who often binds others, is always bound himself." Gill notes the man's haunts in a heathen country "would have rendered him an unclean person, if he had not been possessed with an unclean spirit."

Henry (under v.1) drew out the self-destruction: "this wretched creature was night and day in the mountains and in the tombs, crying, and cutting himself with stones, either bemoaning his own deplorable case, or in a rage and indignation against heaven. Men in frenzies often wound and destroy themselves; what is a man, when reason is dethroned and Satan enthroned?" He set the two voices against each other: "The voice of God is, Do thyself no harm; the voice of Satan is, Do thyself all the harm thou canst."

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which completes the picture of the demoniac's misery (vv.3-5). The witnesses agree: the man's ceaseless crying and self-laceration, night and day among the tombs and mountains, display the cruelty of Satan toward those he holds captive — a master who drives his slaves to torment and destroy themselves. The self-cutting is the very signature of demonic bondage, the opposite of the gospel's "Do thyself no harm" (Acts 16:28). Yet, as Clarke notes, even this dreadful power has its limit: "the devil himself has his chain"; he can go no further than God permits. The man whose self-destruction knew no rest by day or night would, at Christ's coming, find rest at last — sitting at the Deliverer's feet, restored to his right mind (v.15).
6:
"But when he saw Jesus afar off, he ran and worshipped him." The witnesses note the sudden change in the raging man, who ran not in fury but in reverence. Gill: "such was the power of Christ over the devils in him, that though sore against their wills, they obliged him to move on speedily towards him." Barnes: "Bowed down before him; rendered him homage. This was an acknowledgment of his power, and of his control over fallen spirits." Clarke: "Did him homage; compelled thereto by the power of God. How humiliating to Satan, thus to be obliged to acknowledge the superiority of Christ!" Robertson, with Swete: "At first perhaps with hostile intentions. The onrush of the naked yelling maniac must have tried the newly recovered confidence of the Twelve. We can imagine their surprise when, on approaching, he threw himself on his knees."

The Reformed Calvin clarifies the order of events and the compulsion: the demons "did not come of their own accord into the presence of Christ, but were drawn by a secret exercise of his authority. As they had formerly been accustomed to carry men off, in furious violence... so now a superior power compels them to appear reluctantly at the tribunal of their judge. Hence we infer, that the whole of Satan's kingdom is subject to the authority of Christ." The devils, "when Christ summons them to appear before him, are not more at their own disposal than were the wretched men whom their tyranny was wont to drive about."

Henry (under v.1) marked the double aspect: "He usually ran upon others with rage, but he ran to Christ with reverence... Even the devil, in this poor creature, was forced to tremble before Christ, and bow to him; or, rather, the poor man came, and worshipped Christ, in a sense of the need he had of his help, the power of Satan in and over him being, for this instant, suspended."

The witnesses agree: the demoniac's running to worship Christ — the same man who ran upon all others with violence — displays the irresistible authority of Christ over the kingdom of darkness. Whether read as the man's own suppressed cry for help or as the demons dragged unwillingly before their judge, the act proclaims that all Satan's power is held in check under Christ. The naked, raging maniac, terror of the whole region, falls at the feet of the Lord — a picture of the helplessness of the powers of darkness before the Son of God, and of the first stirring of deliverance in the captive soul.
7:
"And cried with a loud voice, and said, What have I to do with thee, Jesus, thou Son of the most high God? I adjure thee by God, that thou torment me not." The witnesses note the demon's forced confession, its dread, and its attempt to turn Christ's own weapon against him. Clarke explains the idiom: "What is it to thee and me, or why dost thou trouble thyself with me?" Robertson: "The demoniac puts Jesus on oath (two accusatives)... He calls Jesus here son of the Most High God (cf. Genesis 14:18). Torment me not... The word means to test metals and then to test one by torture."

The grammatical-historical Ellicott gives the history of the name "the Most High God," the first occurrence in the New Testament. It "belonged to the earliest stage of the patriarchal worship," used by Melchizedek (Genesis 14:18), Balaam (Numbers 24:16), and Moses (Deuteronomy 32:8), and was "one of the words which... helped to place the Gentile and the Jew on a common ground." It "seems to have been frequently used as a formula of exorcism; and this, perhaps, accounts for its being met with here... as coming from the lips of demoniacs." The verb "I adjure thee" is "that from which comes our word exorcise" — the demoniac "seeks in some sense to exorcise, though it is in the language not of command, but entreaty."

Henry (under v.1) had drawn out the four elements: the demon "calls God the most high God"; "owns Jesus to be the Son of God" — yet "it is no strange thing to hear the best words drop from the worst mouths"; "disowns any design against Christ"; and "deprecates his wrath... though I know I am sentenced, yet let me not be sent to the chains of darkness." Cyril, on-topic, lists the demons among the many witnesses to Christ: "devils saying, What have we to do with Thee, Jesus? we know Thee, who Thou art, the Holy One of God."

The witnesses agree: the demon, dreading torment, confesses Christ as Son of the Most High God and tries to bind him by oath — turning the exorcist's own formula into a plea. The confession is true but loveless, wrung out by fear, as the unclean spirit recognizes its conqueror and judge. The very name "Most High God," familiar on Gentile and exorcist lips, is forced from the demon's mouth as an unwilling testimony to the deity of the One who has come to set the captive free.
8:
"For he said unto him, Come out of the man, thou unclean spirit." The witnesses note that this command of Christ was the occasion of the demon's outcry (v.7), and they observe the imperfect tense that shows Christ had been repeatedly ordering the spirit out. Ellicott: "The Greek verb is in the imperfect tense, he was saying, as though the demoniac had interrupted our Lord even while the words were in the act of being uttered." Robertson agrees: "For he had been saying (progressive imperfect). Jesus had already repeatedly ordered the demon to come out of the man whereat the demon made his outcry to Jesus and protested." He notes (with Matthew 8:29) that the demons did not want to go back to the abyss "right now. That was their real home, but they did not wish to return to the place of torment just now."

Ellicott marks a further point: "It is noticeable that our Lord first speaks as if the men were oppressed by a single demon only, and that it is in the answer of the man himself that we learn that their name was Legion." Christ's command "Come out" was addressed to the unclean spirit as one, before the multitude within was revealed (v.9). Gill notes the command "drew out the confession from him, that he was superior to him, and therefore became his supplicant."

The verse, though brief, marks the decisive word of authority. Henry (under v.1) had drawn out its force: "He made the man desirous to be relieved, when he enabled him to run, and worship him, and then put forth his power for his relief. If Christ work in us heartily to pray for a deliverance from Satan, he will work for us that deliverance." The command "Come out of the man" is the word of the Deliverer, and the design of the whole gospel: "Come out of the man, thou unclean spirit, that the Holy Spirit may enter, may take possession of the heart, and have dominion in it."

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which records the command that provoked the demon's plea. The witnesses agree: Christ's authoritative word, "Come out," repeatedly pressed upon the unclean spirit, both occasioned the demon's terrified outcry and set in motion the deliverance. The imperfect tense reveals the conflict in progress — Christ commanding, the demon resisting and pleading — until the superior power of the Lord prevails. The command is the heart of the exorcism and a figure of the gospel's purpose: to drive out the unclean spirit that the Holy Spirit may dwell. What no chain could do (vv.3-4), the word of Christ accomplishes.
9:
"And he asked him, What is thy name? And he answered, saying, My name is Legion: for we are many." The witnesses note why Christ asked, and the dreadful import of the answer. Gill: Christ asked "not for his own sake; for he was not ignorant of his name... but partly, that it might be known what a miserable condition this poor man was in, being infested with such a large company of devils; and partly, that his own pity and power in delivering him, might be more manifest." Robertson: "A full Roman legion had 6,826 men... This may not have been a full legion, for Mark 5:13 notes that the number of hogs was about two thousand." Lightfoot notes that among the Jews "a legion" was used "to express a great number," and "they reckoned all the legions for unclean."

The Reformed Calvin draws the doctrine: "The devil was compelled by Christ to pronounce this word, that he might more fully display the greatness and excellence of his grace... What compassion then was it, to rescue from so many deaths a man who was more than a thousand times ruined!" He refutes a common error: "This passage refutes also the common error... that every man is attacked by his own particular devil. On the contrary, Scripture plainly declares, that, just as it pleases God, one devil is sometimes sent to punish a whole nation, and at other times many devils are permitted to punish one man." The lesson: "how wretched a creature man is, when he is deprived of the divine protection."

The puritan Henry (under v.1) drew out the legion's nature: the devils are "military powers" warring against God; "numerous" — "what multitudes of apostate spirits"; "unanimous" — "many devils, and yet but one legion," which refutes the Pharisees' charge that Satan is divided against himself; "powerful" — "Who can stand before a legion?"; and ordered, with "principalities, and powers." Wesley: "all these seem to have been under one commander, who accordingly speaks all along."

The witnesses agree: Christ's question drew from the demon the dreadful confession of his multitude — a legion in one poor man — both to display the misery of the captive and to magnify the compassion and power of the Deliverer who freed him from so vast an army. The answer refutes the notion of one private devil per man, and reveals the numerous, unanimous, ordered hosts of darkness — against which, Henry adds, "in the Lord, and in the power of his might, we shall be able to stand, though there are legions of them."
10:
"And he besought him much that he would not send them away out of the country." The witnesses note the demons' earnest entreaty and what it reveals of their subjection to Christ and their malice toward men. Gill: "the devil that was at the head of this legion... entreated Jesus with great earnestness. This shows the authority Christ has over the devils, and their subjection to him; they are not only obliged to quit their former possession, when he gives orders, but they cannot go any where else, or where they would, without his leave." The man they possessed could not be bound, "yet these themselves are bound and held in chains, and cannot move without Christ's permission."

Ellicott reads the psychology: the words are "singularly significant of the state of the demoniac as half-conscious of his own personal being, and half-identifying himself with the disturbing demoniac forces which were tormenting him, and yet in so doing were leading him to look on the great Healer as his tormentor." Clarke notes the demons' attachment to the place: "as this is supposed to have been a heathen district... the demons might consider themselves in their own territories; and probably they could act there with less restraint than they could do in a country where the worship of God was established."

The Reformed Calvin interprets the request (which Luke gives as not to be sent "into the deep"): "I rather view it as referring to their rage for doing mischief. As the devils have no other object than to prowl among men, like lions in search of prey, they are grieved at being plunged into the deep, where they will have no opportunity of injuring and ruining men... they manifest their disposition to be such, that there is nothing which they more eagerly desire than the destruction of mankind."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the demons' earnest plea not to be sent out of the country displays both their complete subjection to Christ — who alone determines where they may go — and their insatiable desire to remain among men to do them harm. They who could not be bound in the man are themselves bound by Christ's word, unable to move without his leave. Their dread of being banished or sent to the deep is, as Calvin sees, the dread of being deprived of their prey; their whole disposition is bent on the destruction of mankind. The verse thus reveals the malice of the powers of darkness and, set against it, the absolute authority of Christ, whose permission they must beg even to do mischief.
11:
"Now there was there nigh unto the mountains a great herd of swine feeding." The brief verse introduces the herd of swine that the demons would request to enter (v.12). Gill discusses the location — "the mountains of Galaad... or the mountains that surrounded Tiberias" — and notes the textual variants ("the mountain," singular, in some copies). Barnes cross-references the law forbidding swine to the Jews (Leviticus 11:7-8; Deuteronomy 14:8), the unclean animal being a fit object for the unclean spirits.

The presence of a great herd of swine in this region confirms its heathen character. Lightfoot (under v.1) had noted that Gadara "was a city of heathens (hence it is less marvel if there were swine among them)." Jews were forbidden to keep swine; the herd belonged either to Gentiles or to apostate Jews. Henry (under v.1) suggested the demons abode in that country because "there were many apostate Jews, who had thrown themselves out of the covenant of God, and had thereby given Satan power over them."

The swine, ceremonially unclean and naturally given to wallowing in the mire, are, in the witnesses' reading, the fit habitation the demons sought. Remigius (under v.1) had explained the demons' choice: "they desired to be sent into the swine, because no animal is more unclean than a hog, and devils always delight in filthiness." Henry: "Those that, like the swine, delight in the mire of sensual lusts, are fit habitations for Satan, and are, like Babylon, the hold of every foul spirit" (Revelation 18:2), "as pure souls are habitations of the Holy Spirit."

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which simply sets the scene for the demons' request and its consequence (vv.12-13). The witnesses agree: the great herd of swine feeding near the mountains both confirms the heathen setting of the miracle and provides the demons their desired object. The unclean animals, forbidden to Israel and given to the mire, are the fitting refuge the unclean spirits sought when driven from the man. The detail prepares for the dramatic destruction of the herd (v.13), by which the malice and power of the demons were made plain to all the country, and the Gadarenes were given occasion to seek the One who alone could control them — though, sadly, they chose rather to lose their Saviour than their swine (v.17).
12:
"And all the devils besought him, saying, Send us into the swine, that we may enter into them." The brief verse records the demons' request to be permitted to enter the herd. Gill: "The whole legion of them, not only their chief, in the name of the rest, but all of them earnestly entreated him; they were all humble supplicants, not from love, but fear, and with a view to do mischief." Barnes cross-references Job 1:10-12; 2:5-6, where Satan must obtain God's permission before afflicting Job — the demons here likewise unable to act without Christ's leave.

The request to enter the swine the witnesses read as the demons' continued malice. Having been driven from the man, they seek another habitation, and choose the unclean swine — both as a fit refuge for unclean spirits (Remigius, under v.1: "devils always delight in filthiness") and, as Henry (under v.1) suggested, with the design of doing greater mischief: "by destroying which they hoped to do more mischief to the souls of all the people in the country, than they could by entering into the body of any particular person." For by the loss of the swine, the demons knew the Gadarenes might be turned against Christ — as indeed happened (v.17).

The demons' entreaty also displays, once more, their subjection to Christ. They cannot enter even the swine without his permission; the powers that could not be bound in the man must beg leave of the Lord. Calvin (under v.6) had drawn the lesson: "the whole of Satan's kingdom is subject to the authority of Christ"; the devils "are not more at their own disposal than were the wretched men whom their tyranny was wont to drive about."

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which records the demons' petition. The witnesses agree: the demons, driven from the man, beg to enter the swine — a request springing from fear, not love, and aimed at further mischief, yet wholly subject to Christ's permission. They are "humble supplicants" only in the sense that they must entreat the One whose authority they cannot resist. The request reveals both the malice of the powers of darkness, ever seeking a habitation from which to harm, and their complete subjection to Christ, who holds even the demons in check. Their petition, granted (v.13), would result in the destruction of the herd — a vivid demonstration to all the country of the fury the demons would have wrought on men, had not Christ restrained them.
13:
"And forthwith Jesus gave them leave. And the unclean spirits went out, and entered into the swine: and the herd ran violently down a steep place into the sea, (they were about two thousand;) and were choked in the sea." The witnesses face the difficulty of why Christ permitted the destruction of the herd, and note Mark's precise number. Ellicott: "They were about two thousand. The number, which is peculiar to St. Mark, may be noted as another instance of his graphic accuracy in detail." Robertson: "These words present the crucial difficulty for interpreters as to why Jesus allowed the demons to enter the hogs and destroy them instead of sending them back to the abyss. Certainly it was better for hogs to perish than men, but this loss of property raises a difficulty of its own akin to the problem of tornadoes and earthquakes." He pictures the scene: "Imperfect tense picturing graphically the disappearance of pig after pig in the sea."

The fathers (under v.1) gave the purpose of the permission. The Pseudo-Chrysostom commentary: Christ "wished to shew publicly the fury which devils entertain against men, and that they would inflict much worse things upon men, if they were not hindered by Divine power; because... His compassion would not allow this to be shewn on men, He permitted them to enter into the swine, that on them the fury and power of the devils might be made known." Bede: "He gave them leave, that by the killing of the swine, the salvation of men might be furthered."

Henry (under v.1) drew the lesson: "Thus he would let the Gadarenes see what powerful spiteful enemies devils are, that they might thereby be induced to make him their Friend." And he noted the mercy to the man: "The man they possessed did only cut himself, for God had said, He is in your hands, only save his life. But thereby it appeared, that, if he had not been so restrained, the poor man would have drowned himself." The destruction of the swine showed what the demons would have done to the man.

The witnesses agree: Christ's permission, granted "forthwith," resulted in the violent destruction of the whole herd, displaying publicly the murderous fury of the demons — a fury they would have vented on men, had not divine power restrained them. The loss of the swine, troubling as it is to interpreters, served a merciful end: to make manifest the malice of the powers of darkness, to magnify the deliverance of the man, and to call the Gadarenes to seek the One who alone could control such enemies.
14:
"And they that fed the swine fled, and told it in the city, and in the country. And they went out to see what it was that was done." The witnesses note the flight of the herdsmen and the spreading report that drew the people out. Gill: "Not the owners, but the keepers of them, the swine herds, fled; being astonished at the power of Christ, affrighted at the noise of the devils, and terrified at the sight and loss of the swine." Robertson notes Mark adds "in the country" to "the city": "In the fields and in the city as the excited men ran they told the tale of the destruction of the hogs. They came to see — all the city came out (Matthew), they went out to see (Luke)."

Lightfoot discusses the phrase "in the country" (literally "in the fields"), explaining from rabbinic usage that "dwellers in the field" were "inhabitants of the villages" or "private men who dwell in the fields... in houses scattered here and there." The report thus spread both to the city and to the scattered country dwellings, drawing a crowd to the scene.

The herdsmen's flight and report set in motion the gathering of the people (v.15). The loss of the swine — a calamity to the owners — became the occasion of bringing the whole country to Christ. As the Pseudo-Chrysostom commentary (under v.1) had noted: "The necessity of their loss, however, brought these men to the Saviour; for frequently when God makes men suffer loss in their possessions, He confers a benefit on their souls."

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which records the herdsmen's flight and the spreading news. The witnesses agree: the keepers of the swine, terrified at the loss and the power they had witnessed, fled and spread the report through city and country, drawing the people out to see. The destruction of the herd, far from being hidden, was published abroad by its own keepers, so that the whole region came to behold both the loss and the delivered man (v.15). The loss of property, troubling as it was, became God's means of bringing the people face to face with Christ — though, sadly, most would prize their swine above their Saviour (v.17). The verse marks the transition from the miracle to its public reckoning, as the country gathers to judge what manner of man had come among them.
15:
"And they come to Jesus, and see him that was possessed with the devil, and had the legion, sitting, and clothed, and in his right mind: and they were afraid." The witnesses dwell on the transformation of the demoniac and the fear it produced. Barnes: "There could be no doubt of the reality of this miracle. The man had been well known... To see him all at once peaceful, and calm, and rational, was proof that it was the power of God only that had done it. They were afraid — they were awed, as in the presence of God." Robertson: "they had all been afraid of the man, but there he was sitting clothed and in his right mind... At the feet of Jesus, Luke adds. For a long time he had worn no clothes." Ellicott notes the antithesis "had the legion... is given by St. Mark only" — pointing the contrast between the man's past and present state.

The Reformed Calvin draws the sober lesson from their fear: "We have here a striking proof that not all who perceive the hand of God profit as they ought to do by yielding themselves to him in sincere godliness. Having seen the miracle, the Gadarenes were afraid, because the majesty of God shone brightly in Christ. So far they did right; but now that they send him out of their territories, what could have been done worse?" Their fear, Luke shows, was awe at the divine power, not merely dread of further loss: "his face is terrible, so long as they contemplate him as a Judge, and not as a Father." Calvin draws the contemporary application: "so long as men believe that the kingdom of God is opposed to their interest... they are prepossessed by a depraved and carnal fear, and have no relish for his grace."

Henry (under v.1) marked the proof: "when Satan was cast out, he came to himself, and was his own man presently. Note, Those who are grave and sober, and live by rule... make it appear that by the power of Christ the devil's power is broken in their souls."

The witnesses agree: the sight of the once-raging demoniac now sitting, clothed, and in his right mind at the feet of Jesus was undeniable proof of the divine power that had wrought the change — and it filled the people with awe. Yet their fear, awe at the majesty of God in Christ, did not ripen into faith; it would soon turn (v.17) into a request that he depart, when they reckoned the cost in swine. The transformed man is the picture of the soul delivered by Christ — calm, clothed, restored, seated at the Deliverer's feet; the fearful Gadarenes, of those who perceive God's power yet, prizing their interest above his grace, will not receive him.
16:
"And they that saw it told them how it befell to him that was possessed with the devil, and also concerning the swine." The brief verse — with only Gill in the Catena — records the testimony of the eyewitnesses to the gathered crowd. Gill identifies them: "Not the keepers of the swine, for they were fled and gone; but others that were eyewitnesses of the whole affair, who lived in houses hard by, or were working in the fields; or the disciples of Christ."

The eyewitnesses report two things: the deliverance of the demoniac and the destruction of the swine. Both were laid before the assembled people — the miracle of mercy and the loss of property. Henry (under v.1) had noted how the people responded to each: "When they saw how wonderfully the poor man was cured, they hence conceived a veneration for Christ... But when they found that their swine were lost, they thence conceived a dislike of Christ." The double report set before them both the power of Christ to save and the cost to their possessions, and they would weigh the one against the other.

The telling of the eyewitnesses establishes the reality of the miracle on independent testimony. Those who had seen the whole affair — the man's former condition, his deliverance, and the drowning of the herd — bore witness to the crowd who had come out to see. The account was not hearsay but the report of those who had beheld it, confirming both the cure and its dramatic accompaniment.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which records the eyewitness testimony. The witnesses agree on its place in the narrative: those who had seen the deliverance and the destruction of the swine reported both to the gathered people, laying before them the full evidence of what Christ had done. The report would provoke the fateful decision of v.17 — for, hearing of the loss of their swine, the Gadarenes would prize their property above their Deliverer and beg him to depart. The verse thus stands at the hinge between the miracle and its rejection: the testimony is clear, the evidence undeniable, yet the people, weighing swine against the Saviour, would make the wrong choice. The eyewitnesses' faithful report leaves the country without excuse, having heard plainly both the mercy and the power of the One who had come among them.
17:
"And they began to pray him to depart out of their coasts." The witnesses note the tragic request of the Gadarenes, who valued their swine above their Saviour. Gill: "the inhabitants of the city of Gadara, and of the villages round about, very earnestly importuned him" to leave. Robertson draws out the motive: "Here in Decapolis pagan influence was strong and the owners of the hogs cared more for the loss of their property than for the healing of the wild demoniac. In the clash between business and spiritual welfare business came first with them as often today... They feared the power of Jesus and wanted no further interference with their business affairs."

The request to depart is, for the witnesses, the saddest turn in the narrative. Henry (under v.1) lamented it: "they wished to have rather his room than his company; they prayed him to depart out of their coasts, for they think not any good he can do them sufficient to make them amends for the loss of so many swine." And he drew the application: "Thus they do, who, rather than let go a base lust, will throw away their interest in Christ, and their expectations from him." They should rather have reasoned: "If he has such a power as this over devils and all creatures, it is good having him our Friend."

The witnesses note the bitter irony: the demons had begged not to be sent out of the country (v.10), and the Gadarenes now beg Christ to leave it — so that the demons have their wish, and the people drive away their only Deliverer. Henry: "Now the devils had what they would have; for by no handle do these evil spirits more effectually manage sinful souls than by that of the love of the world."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the Gadarenes, reckoning the loss of their swine greater than the gift of Christ's presence, begged him to depart — choosing their property over their Saviour, business over spiritual welfare. The request is a standing warning against the love of the world, which would rather lose Christ than a base interest. The same Jesus who had once been driven from Nazareth (Robertson) is here asked to leave Decapolis; and he does not force his presence on those who reject it, but enters the boat to depart (v.18) — leaving behind, as his witness, the delivered man (vv.19-20). The verse marks the country's tragic choice and the mercy that, even in departing, leaves a herald behind.
18:
"And when he was come into the ship, he that had been possessed with the devil prayed him that he might be with him." The brief verse records the delivered man's desire to follow Christ. Gill: "at the request of these men, who were unworthy of his presence... he turned back to the sea side again; and as he was about to take ship," the healed man begged to come with him. Robertson: "The man began to beseech him before it was too late."

The contrast is striking: the Gadarenes beg Christ to depart (v.17), while the delivered man begs to go with him. Henry (under v.1) drew it out: the man "desired that he might go along with Christ, perhaps for fear lest the evil spirit should again seize him; or, rather, that he might receive instruction from him, being unwilling to stay among those heathenish people that desired him to depart. Those that are freed from the evil spirit, cannot but covet acquaintance and fellowship with Christ."

The man's desire to be with Christ is the natural response of one who has been delivered. Having been freed from the legion, he clings to his Deliverer, unwilling to be parted from him and perhaps fearful of returning to his former bondage. His prayer "that he might be with him" expresses the love and gratitude of the rescued soul — and stands in pointed contrast to the country's rejection. Where the Gadarenes saw only the loss of their swine, the healed man saw the worth of Christ's presence above all.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which records the man's request. The witnesses agree: the delivered demoniac, in contrast to his countrymen, longed to stay with Christ — the proper instinct of the rescued soul, which covets fellowship with its Deliverer. Yet Christ would not suffer it (v.19), but sent him home to witness, giving him a higher service than the one he asked. The man's desire "to be with him" is the desire of every soul Christ has freed; and though here it was not granted in the form he wished, it was answered in a better way — for to be sent by Christ to proclaim his mercy is itself a form of being with him, and a sharing in his work. The verse thus prepares for the commission of vv.19-20, in which the man's love is redirected from following to witnessing.
19:
"Howbeit Jesus suffered him not, but saith unto him, Go home to thy friends, and tell them how great things the Lord hath done for thee, and hath had compassion on thee." The witnesses note why Christ declined the man's request and the commission he gave instead. Gill: Christ was "as able to preserve him from those evil spirits, when absent, as present; and besides, to take him along with him, would look like ostentation." Barnes lists the reasons: to leave him "as a conclusive evidence of his power to work miracles"; to show "that the cure was complete"; but "the probable reason is, that he desired to restore him to his family and friends... unwilling to delay the joy of his friends."

The grammatical voices note the contrast with Christ's usual practice. Robertson: "In Galilee Jesus had several times forbidden the healed to tell what he had done for them because of the undue excitement and misunderstanding. But here it was different. There was no danger of too much enthusiasm for Christ in this environment." The man is given "the hardest task of all, to go home and witness there for Christ." On "the Lord," Ellicott notes (with Luke 8:39's "God") that the word "must be taken in its Old Testament sense, as referring, not to the Lord Jesus, but to the Father."

The commission emphasizes compassion as much as power. Henry (under v.1): "He must take particular notice rather of Christ's pity than of his power, for that is it which especially he glories in; he must tell them what compassion the Lord had had on him in his misery." The man is sent home to his own folk, to witness where he was best known, that Christ might be honoured and his neighbours edified.

The witnesses agree: Christ declined the man's request to follow, and instead sent him home to bear witness to his family and friends — a service better suited to that region, where (unlike Galilee) there was no danger of excessive enthusiasm. The commission to "tell how great things the Lord hath done" makes the delivered man the first herald of Christ in Decapolis, and bids him glory above all in the Lord's compassion. The verse teaches that the desire to be with Christ may be answered by being sent for Christ; that the rescued soul's truest service may be to witness where it is known; and that the proper theme of such witness is not merely Christ's power but his mercy. The man asked to follow; Christ gave him a flock to feed.
20:
"And he departed, and began to publish in Decapolis how great things Jesus had done for him: and all men did marvel." The witnesses note the man's obedient and joyful witness, and its wide effect. Gill: "He submitted to the will of Christ, though he could gladly have gone with him; he hearkened to his instructions... and he went not only to his own, or his father's house... but he published the account... throughout the whole city." Robertson: "He went off and did as Jesus told him. He heralded or published the story till all over Decapolis men marvelled at what Jesus did, kept on marvelling (imperfect tense). The man had a greater opportunity for Christ right in his home land than anywhere else. They all knew this once wild demoniac who now was a new man in Christ Jesus. Thousands of like cases of conversion under Christ's power have happened in rescue missions in our cities."

A telling detail: Christ had said "tell how great things the Lord hath done" (v.19), and the man "began to publish how great things Jesus had done." Theophylact (under v.1) had marked the humility Christ taught: "He said not, Proclaim all things which I have done to you, but, all that the Lord hath done; do thou also, when thou hast done any good thing, take it not to thyself, but refer it to God." Yet the man, in his witness, identifies "the Lord" with Jesus — a quiet testimony to Christ's deity.

Barnes draws the application: "This was the natural expression of right feeling at being cured of such a calamity. So the desire of sinners freed from sin is to honour Jesus; to ascribe all to his power; and to invite the world to participate in the same salvation."

The witnesses agree: the delivered man, obeying Christ's word, became the first herald of the gospel in Decapolis, publishing throughout the region how great things Jesus had done for him, and all men marvelled. Though forbidden to follow, he served Christ better at home, his very presence — a once-wild demoniac now a new man — a living testimony to the power of the One who had freed him. His witness, identifying "the Lord" of Christ's commission with Jesus himself, quietly proclaims his deity. The verse closes the demoniac narrative with a note of triumph and a pattern for all the rescued: to publish, where they are best known, the great things Christ has done, that others too may marvel and seek the same salvation.
21:
"And when Jesus was passed over again by ship unto the other side, much people gathered unto him: and he was nigh unto the sea." The witnesses note the return to Galilee and the two intertwined miracles that follow — Jairus's daughter and the woman with the issue of blood. The Antiochene and Latin fathers begin the exposition. Theophylact: Jairus "was faithful in part, inasmuch as he fell at the feet of Jesus, but in that he begged of Him to come, he did not shew as much faith as he ought. For he ought to have said, Speak the word only, and my daughter shall be healed." The woman, by contrast, was "most faithful... who hoped for healing from His garments." He reads the woman's flux as human nature wounded by sin, healed by touching "the hem of Christ's garment, that is, His flesh."

Bede draws the great typology that runs through both miracles: "the woman with an issue of blood, cured by the Lord, is the Church gathered together from the nations... But whilst the word of the Lord decreed salvation to Judaea, the people of the Gentiles by an assured hope seized upon the health, promised and prepared for others." The daughter of the ruler, raised after the woman is healed, is the synagogue, saved after "the fulness of the Gentiles has come in, then shall Israel be saved" (Romans 11). Pseudo-Jerome: "Jairus comes after the healing of the woman, because when the fulness of the Gentiles has come in, then shall Israel be saved."

The puritan Henry gives the running exposition (covering vv.21-34): "A despised gospel will cross the water, and go where it will have better entertainment." Jairus, "one of the rulers of the synagogue," came "with great humility and reverence... and with great importunity." The woman "comes clandestinely to steal a cure for herself," and "this cure was wrought by the way." Henry draws the lessons of her case: "It is usual with people not to apply themselves to Christ, till they have tried in vain all other helpers... And he will be found a sure refuge, even to those who make him their last refuge."

The witnesses agree: Christ, returning across the sea, is met by a great crowd, and there begins the double miracle — the ruler's urgent plea for his dying daughter interrupted by the secret, faith-filled touch of the suffering woman. The two are woven together by the Evangelist, and read by the fathers as a figure of salvation's order: first the Gentile Church healed by faith's touch, then Israel raised from death. Jairus's partial faith and the woman's bold faith are set side by side, and both are met by the compassion and power of Christ.
22:
"And, behold, there cometh one of the rulers of the synagogue, Jairus by name; and when he saw him, he fell at his feet." The witnesses note the rank and humility of the suppliant. Wesley explains the office: "To regulate the affairs of every synagogue, there was a council of grave men. Over these was a president, who was termed the ruler of the synagogue. Sometimes there was no more than one ruler in a synagogue." Gill notes he came "having heard of his return, and where he was."

The remarkable thing, the witnesses note, is that a ruler of the synagogue — a man of rank and authority — should fall at the feet of Jesus. Henry (under v.21) drew it out: "It is no less a person than one of the rulers of the synagogue... He addressed himself to Christ, though a ruler, with great humility and reverence; When he saw him, he fell at his feet, giving honour to him as one really greater than he appeared to be." The extremity of his need — a dying daughter — broke through the reserve that often kept the rulers from Christ, and brought him as a humble suppliant to the Master's feet.

That Jairus is named, when many healed persons are not, the fathers noted. The Pseudo-Chrysostom commentary (under v.21): "He has recorded the name on account of the Jews of that time, that it might mark the miracle" — the named, known ruler standing as a witness to the reality of what Christ did.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree on its force: a ruler of the synagogue, a man of standing, came and fell at the feet of Jesus — a striking act of humility and reverence, born of the desperate need of a dying child. (The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown fragment here, on the rending of the temple veil, belongs to Matthew 27, not this verse.) The naming of Jairus marks the miracle as a public, attested event, and his prostration before Christ models the reverence with which all should approach him. Affliction, the witnesses note, often brings to Christ those who in prosperity kept their distance; the dying daughter brought the ruler to the Master's feet. His coming sets in motion the great miracle of vv.35-43, even as it is interrupted by the woman's secret touch — the urgent public plea and the hidden private faith woven together in the one narrative.
23:
"And besought him greatly, saying, My little daughter lieth at the point of death: I pray thee, come and lay thy hands on her, that she may be healed; and she shall live." The witnesses note the tenderness of the father's plea and the extremity of the child's condition. Gill: "Used much importunity with him, and was very urgent in his requests." Robertson catches the affection in the diminutive: "My little daughter. Diminutive of daughter. This little endearing touch in the use of the diminutive is peculiar to Mark." Lightfoot notes the Jewish reckoning of age: "A daughter from her birthday, until she is twelve years old complete, is called little, or a little maid" — and she was just twelve (v.42). Clarke: "The words express much tenderness and concern. Luke observes that she was his only daughter."

On "lieth at the point of death," the grammatical voices note the gravity. Robertson: "Has it in the last stages. Matthew 9:18 has: has just died, Luke she lay a dying." He explains the harmony: Mark and Luke give the condition as Jairus first stated it (at the point of death), Matthew compresses to the final outcome (dead). Ellicott: "Literally, is at the last point; in extremis."

The father's faith, though partial (Theophylact, under v.21, faulted his asking Christ to come rather than merely speak the word), is real and earnest. He believes that if Christ will come and lay his hands on her, "she shall live." Henry (under v.21): "he believes that if Christ will but come, and lay his hands upon her, she will return even from the gates of the grave."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the ruler's plea, full of a father's tender affection (the "little daughter" peculiar to Mark) and of earnest faith, lays before Christ the extremity of the child — at the very point of death — and begs the touch that he believes will heal her. His faith, though it ties Christ's power to his bodily coming and touch, is genuine and importunate; and Christ answers it (v.24) without rebuking its imperfection. The verse sets the stage for the great miracle, even as the delay caused by the woman's touch (vv.25-34) will press the father's faith to its limit, when word comes that the child is dead (v.35). The tender plea of a desperate father, met by the ready compassion of Christ, opens the way to the raising of the dead.
24:
"And Jesus went with him; and much people followed him, and thronged him." The brief verse records Christ's ready compliance and the pressing crowd. Gill: "Immediately without objecting to the weakness of his faith." Robertson: "Went off with him promptly, but a great multitude followed him, was following, kept following (imperfect tense). They thronged him... Were pressing Jesus so that he could hardly move because of the jam, or even to breathe."

Christ's immediate going with Jairus, the witnesses note, displays both his compassion and his readiness to answer faith — even a faith as imperfect as the ruler's. Henry (under v.21): "Christ readily agreed, and went with him." He did not delay, did not rebuke the father's weakness, but at once set out toward the dying child.

The thronging crowd sets the scene for the woman's secret touch (vv.25-29). Pressed on every side, Christ moves slowly through the multitude; and in that press, the suffering woman finds her opportunity to approach unnoticed. The throng that hemmed him in becomes the cover for the hidden act of faith. The witnesses (with Henry, under v.21) note the providence: "this cure was wrought by the way, as he was going to raise the ruler's daughter, and was followed by a crowd. See how Christ improved his time, and lost none of the precious moments of it."

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which records Christ's going and the crowd's pressing. The witnesses agree on its place in the narrative: Christ promptly went with Jairus toward the dying child, while a great multitude thronged and pressed upon him. His ready compliance shows his compassion and his willingness to honour even an imperfect faith; the thronging crowd, which made his progress slow, providentially provided the setting for the woman's secret cure. The delay caused by the press — and soon by the woman's touch (vv.30-34) — would test the father's faith to the utmost, yet would also display, in the woman's healing, a fresh proof of Christ's power to encourage him. The verse thus links the two miracles: Christ on his way to raise the dead, interrupted by the touch that heals the living, both woven into the one journey of mercy through the thronging crowd.
25:
"And a certain woman, which had an issue of blood twelve years." The brief verse introduces the suffering woman whose secret cure interrupts the journey to Jairus's house. Gill notes she "was in the crowd that thronged Jesus, as he passed through the streets of Capernaum," and recounts the tradition reported by Eusebius that she was of Caesarea Philippi, where a bronze monument of her kneeling before Christ was said to stand "until the times of Julian the apostate."

The woman's affliction — an issue of blood for twelve years — was both physically debilitating and ceremonially defiling. Under the law (Leviticus 15:19ff., cited by Barnes), such an issue rendered her unclean, and all she touched unclean, cutting her off from the social and religious life of the people. Henry (under v.21) drew out her "piteous case": the issue "had thrown her, no doubt, into great weakness, had embittered the comfort of her life, and threatened to be her death in a little time."

The duration — twelve years — is significant. Bede (under v.35) noted the symbolic correspondence: the woman had suffered twelve years, and the girl was twelve years old, "because the sinning of unbelievers was contemporary with the beginning of the faith of believers." The long, incurable affliction sets the stage for the display of Christ's power, which would accomplish in a moment what twelve years of physicians could not.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which introduces the woman and her long affliction. The witnesses agree on its place: the suffering woman, afflicted twelve years with an incurable and defiling issue, enters the narrative as Christ moves toward Jairus's house. Her case — chronic, debilitating, ceremonially isolating, beyond the reach of physicians — sets the stage for the secret touch of faith (vv.27-28) and the instant cure (v.29). The twelve years of her suffering, matching the twelve years of the girl's life, are read by the fathers as part of the providential symbolism of the intertwined miracles. The woman who could not be healed by all the physicians' art would be made whole in an instant by touching the garment of Christ — a parable, as the fathers saw, of the Gentile world's healing by faith's touch upon the incarnate Lord.
26:
"And had suffered many things of many physicians, and had spent all that she had, and was nothing bettered, but rather grew worse." The witnesses dwell on the woman's long and fruitless recourse to physicians, drawing on the rabbinic remedies of the day. Gill and Lightfoot list the Talmudic prescriptions for such a flux: "gum of Alexandria... alum... crocus hortensis... given in wine"; and if that failed, "Persian onions" boiled in wine; and if that failed, to "set her in a place where two ways meet, and let her hold a cup of wine in her hand; and let somebody come behind her and affright her, and say, Arise from thy flux." Clarke recounts the same and concludes: "from some of these nostrums it is evident the woman could not be bettered, and from some others it is as evident that she must be made worse."

The grammatical voices draw out the pathos. Robertson: "A pathetic picture of a woman with a chronic case who had tried doctor after doctor. Had spent all that she had... The tragedy of it was that she was nothing bettered, but rather grew worse. Her money was gone, her disease was gaining on her, her one chance came now with Jesus." He notes that "Luke the physician neatly takes care of the physicians" (Luke 8:43), and that "they were not to blame. She had a disease that they did not know how to cure."

Clarke draws the lesson from her extremity: "Man's extremity is God's opportunity. Never could the power and goodness of God be shown in a more difficult and distressful case." Her affliction was inveterate (twelve years), continual, aggravated by the medicines, ruinous to her health and circumstances — "she was therefore a fit patient for the Great Physician."

The witnesses agree: the woman's long, costly, and fruitless recourse to the physicians — twelve years, all her money spent, her condition only worsening — magnifies the cure that Christ would work in an instant. The rabbinic remedies, some useless and some harmful, had left her worse than before; the Great Physician would heal at a touch what all their art could not. Her case is read (with Henry, under v.21) as a picture of the sinner who applies to Christ only after trying every other helper in vain, and finds him "a sure refuge, even to those who make him their last refuge." The extremity of her need was the occasion for the display of Christ's power and grace.
27:
"When she had heard of Jesus, came in the press behind, and touched his garment." The brief verse records the woman's approach in faith. Gill: "When she had heard of Jesus, of the many miracles he had wrought, and cures he had performed, in cases as difficult and desperate as hers... and that he was now passing along the streets." Barnes: "Came in the press behind. In the crowd that pressed upon him. This was done to avoid being noticed. It was an act of faith. She was full of confidence that Jesus was able to heal; but she trembled on account of her conscious unworthiness, thus illustrating the humility and confidence of a sinner coming to God for pardon and life." Clarke: "She had formed her resolution in faith, she executes it, notwithstanding her weakness, with courage."

The woman's coming "behind" and her touching only his garment the witnesses read as the mark of her shy, conscious unworthiness. Chrysostom (under v.21) explained: "according to the law, she was unclean; for this reason she touched Him behind, and not in front, for that she durst not do, but only ventured to touch the hem of His garment. It was not however the hem of the garment, but her frame of mind that made her whole." Her ceremonial uncleanness (Leviticus 15) made her ashamed to approach openly; she hid in the crowd and reached out in secret.

Yet her secrecy was joined to a bold faith. She believed that even to touch his garment would heal her (v.28). The combination — timid in approach, bold in faith — illustrates, as Barnes notes, "the humility and confidence of a sinner coming to God." She came behind, hidden, unworthy; yet she came, and she touched, and she was made whole.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the woman, having heard of Christ's power, pressed through the crowd and touched his garment from behind — an act of faith mingled with shy, conscious unworthiness. Her ceremonial uncleanness made her shrink from an open approach, yet her confidence in Christ's power drove her to reach out in secret. The touch, as Chrysostom notes, was not in itself the means of healing — "it was her frame of mind that made her whole." Her stealthy, faith-filled approach is a model of the sinner who, conscious of unworthiness yet confident of Christ's mercy, comes humbly and is received. The secret touch would not remain secret, for Christ would draw her forward (vv.30-33) to confess and be confirmed.
28:
"For she said, If I may touch but his clothes, I shall be whole." The brief verse records the faith that moved the woman to her secret touch. Gill: "Within herself, such were her thoughts, and so great her faith." Robertson: "She was timid and shy from her disease and did not wish to attract attention. So she crept up in the crowd and touched the hem or border of his garment according to Matthew 9:20 and Luke 8:44."

The woman's confidence is remarkable. Henry (under v.21) marked it: "she said within herself, though it doth not appear that she was encouraged by any preceding instance to say it, If I may but touch his clothes, I shall be whole. She believed that he cured, not as a prophet, by virtue derived from God, but as the Son of God, by a virtue inherent in himself." Without precedent to guide her, she reasoned that even the touch of his garment would suffice — a faith that grasped, however dimly, the divine power resident in Christ himself.

Her faith, the witnesses note, was suited to her case. Henry: "Her case was such as she could not in modesty tell him publicly, as others did their grievances, and therefore a private cure was what she wished for, and her faith was suited to her case." The secret touch matched her secret affliction; she sought a hidden cure for a shameful disease, and her faith, though mixed with some superstition perhaps (Robertson, under v.30, allows "there was superstition in the woman's mind"), laid hold of Christ's power.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which records the woman's inward resolution of faith. The witnesses agree: her confident persuasion — "If I may touch but his clothes, I shall be whole" — expresses a faith that, without any precedent to encourage it, grasped the inherent power of Christ to heal even through the touch of his garment. Her faith was suited to her case: a private cure for a private grief, a secret touch for a shameful disease. Though perhaps mingled with imperfect notions, it laid hold of the true power of Christ, and was honoured. The verse reveals the inner spring of her action — a bold persuasion of Christ's healing virtue — which Christ would not leave hidden but would draw into the open (vv.30-34), that her faith might be commended and confirmed, and that the cure she had "stolen" might be openly ratified by the word of the Healer.
29:
"And straightway the fountain of her blood was dried up; and she felt in her body that she was healed of that plague." The witnesses note the instant, complete cure and the woman's consciousness of it. Gill explains the phrase from Jewish usage: "It was usual with the Jews to call the womb... a fountain... they called it the fountain of her blood... and sometimes use the same phrase of the drying up of it." Ellicott marks Mark's graphic touch: "She felt in her body. Another graphic and therefore characteristic touch, giving not only the fact, but the woman's consciousness of it." Robertson: "She knew, the verb means. She said to herself, I am healed. It was a vivid moment of joy for her. The plague or scourge was a whip used in flagellations... It is an old word that was used for afflictions regarded as a scourge from God."

The cure was instantaneous and total — "straightway" (Mark's characteristic word) the issue dried up, and she felt the change in her body. Henry (under v.21) drew out the proof: "The flux of blood was dried up, and she felt herself perfectly well all over her, as well as ever she was in her life, in an instant; by this it appears that the cure was altogether miraculous; for those that in such cases are cured by natural means, recover their strength slowly and gradually... but as for God, his work is perfect."

The woman's felt healing is, for the witnesses, both the reward of her faith and the occasion of Christ's inquiry. She knew in her body that the twelve-year affliction was gone; and that very moment, Christ knew in himself that power had gone out of him (v.30). The hidden touch had its hidden effect — but neither would remain hidden long.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the woman's secret touch of faith brought an instant, complete, and consciously-felt cure — the twelve-year fountain of blood dried up at once, and she knew in her body that she was healed of her plague. The instantaneousness and completeness of the cure (no gradual recovery) mark it as miraculous, the perfect work of God. Her felt healing rewarded her faith and matched Christ's own consciousness that power had gone forth (v.30). The word "plague" (literally scourge) frames her long affliction as a chastening from which Christ now released her. The hidden cure, felt in her body, would soon be drawn into the open by Christ's question (v.30), that her faith might be honoured and her healing confirmed by his word.
30:
"And Jesus, immediately knowing in himself that virtue had gone out of him, turned him about in the press, and said, Who touched my clothes?" The witnesses note Christ's consciousness of the power that went forth and the purpose of his question. Gill: "As soon as ever the woman had touched his garments, and had a cure, Christ, who knew all things in his Spirit, or divine nature... knew what was done... though, as not without his knowledge, so neither without his will, and entirely by his power." Ellicott explains the term: "The word virtue is used in the old medical sense, the power or force which brings about a certain definite result... here, not less than in Luke 5:17, with a like technical precision, for the supernatural power that, as it were, flowed out at the touch of faith."

Robertson probes the moment: "Jesus felt in his own consciousness... I felt in myself the power from me go." He notes the scholarly debate "whether Jesus healed the woman by conscious will or by unconscious response to her appeal," but concludes: "What we do know is that Jesus was conscious of the going out of power from himself." He guards against superstition: "There was no magic in the garments of Jesus. Perhaps there was superstition in the woman's mind, but Jesus honoured her darkened faith as in the case of Peter's shadow and Paul's handkerchief."

On the question "Who touched my clothes?" Barnes explains it was not for information: "He said this, not to obtain information, for he had healed her, and must have known on whom the blessing was conferred; but he did it, that the woman might herself make a confession of the whole matter, by which the power of her faith and the greatness of the miracle might be manifested, to the praise of God." The Pseudo-Chrysostom commentary (under v.21) had distinguished the touch of faith from the press of the crowd: "the crowds who throng Me cannot be said to touch Me, for they do not come near to Me in thought and in faith."

The witnesses agree: Christ, conscious of the healing power that had gone forth at the woman's touch, asked "Who touched my clothes?" — not to learn what he already knew, but to draw the woman forward, that her faith might be confessed and the miracle made manifest to the glory of God. The "virtue" that went out was the supernatural power of the divine Healer, flowing at the touch of faith; and Christ, distinguishing the faith-touch from the careless press of the crowd, singled out the one who had truly touched him. His question turns the secret cure into an open testimony.
31:
"And his disciples said unto him, Thou seest the multitude thronging thee, and sayest thou, Who touched me?" The brief verse records the disciples' surprise at what seemed an odd question in a pressing crowd. Gill notes they spoke "after the crowd that were about him denied that any of them had touched him." Robertson: "The disciples were amazed at the sensitiveness of Jesus to the touch of the crowd. They little understood the drain on Jesus from all this healing that pulled at his heart-strings and exhausted his nervous energy even though the Son of God. He had the utmost human sympathy."

The disciples' protest, the witnesses note, sprang from a natural but mistaken view. To them, the question seemed needless: in a crowd pressing on every side, multitudes were touching him. Henry (under v.21) marked their manner: "not without a show of rudeness and indecency, almost ridiculed his question... As if it had been an improper question." They did not understand the difference between the careless touch of the throng and the faith-touch of the woman.

Clarke draws out that difference: "Many touch Jesus who are not healed by him: the reason is, they do not do it by faith, through a sense of their wants, and a conviction of his ability and willingness to save them. Faith conveys the virtue of Christ into the soul, and spiritual health is the immediate consequence of this received virtue." The crowd thronged him; the woman touched him. The one was mere physical contact; the other was the contact of faith, which alone draws healing virtue from Christ.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which records the disciples' protest. The witnesses agree on its lesson: the disciples, seeing only the pressing crowd, thought Christ's question needless — for many were thronging him. But Christ knew the difference between the throng's careless press and the woman's faith-touch. The verse draws out the great distinction, urged by Clarke: many touch Christ outwardly who receive nothing, because they touch without faith; the woman touched in faith and was made whole. So in every age, multitudes press around the gospel and the means of grace, yet only those who touch Christ by faith draw healing virtue from him. The disciples' misunderstanding gives occasion for this lesson, and prepares for the woman's coming forward (vv.32-33), when the difference between the crowd and the believer is made plain.
32:
"And he looked round about to see her that had done this thing." The brief verse records Christ's searching gaze through the crowd to find the woman. Gill: "the press and throng of people, on every side of him; though he knew very well where she stood, who had done the thing, and had received the cure." Ellicott marks the tense: "The tense of the Greek verb implies a continued looking." Robertson: "He kept looking around to find out. The answer of Jesus to the protest of the disciples was this scrutinizing gaze... Jesus knew the difference between touch and touch."

The searching look the witnesses read not as the gaze of one seeking information — for Christ knew well who had touched him (Gill) — but as the means of drawing the woman forward. Henry (under v.21): "Christ passed by the affront, and looks around to see her that had done this thing; not that he might blame her for her presumption, but that he might commend and encourage her faith, and by his own act and deed might warrant and confirm the cure, and ratify to her that which she had surreptitiously obtained." The look was tender, not stern — the look of one seeking to honour faith, not to rebuke it.

Christ's continued looking around, his scrutinizing gaze through the press, was his answer to the disciples' protest (v.31). They had thought the question needless; he, knowing the difference between the throng's touch and the faith-touch, would not let the matter rest until the woman was found. His gaze sought her out, that she might come forward and confess (v.33).

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree on its sense: Christ, knowing well who had touched him, kept looking round through the crowd to draw the woman forth — not to inform himself, nor to rebuke her, but to commend her faith and confirm her cure. His searching gaze, his answer to the disciples' protest, would not rest until the one who had touched him in faith was singled out from the careless throng. The look was the means by which the secret act of faith was brought into the open, that it might be honoured and ratified by Christ's own word (v.34). As Henry notes, "secret acts of faith, as well as secret acts of sin, are known to the Lord Jesus, and are under his eye." The verse marks the gentle persistence of Christ in drawing forth and confirming the hidden faith of his people.
33:
"But the woman fearing and trembling, knowing what was done in her, came and fell down before him, and told him all the truth." The witnesses note the woman's fear, her confession, and the lesson of both. Gill explores her fears: "Lest she should be reproved, and suffer the penalties of the law, for appearing in public during the time of her uncleanness; or that Christ was displeased with her... or lest he should recall it." He draws the parallel to the believer's fears after conversion: "after they have had the pardon of their sins, and are cured of their diseases, they are not without their fears and tremblings, though there is no just reason for them: they fear where no fear is." Robertson: "These participles vividly portray this woman who had tried to hide in the crowd. She had heard Christ's question and felt his gaze. She had to come and confess... Secrecy was no longer possible. She told the pitiful tale of chronic misery."

Henry (under v.21) drew out the woman's trembling and its remedy: "Christ's patients are often trembling, when they have reason to be triumphing. She might have come boldly, knowing what was done in her; yet, knowing that, she fears and trembles." The remedy: "There is nothing better for those that fear and tremble, than to throw themselves at the feet of the Lord Jesus; to humble themselves before him, and refer themselves to him." Her confession — telling "all the truth" — Henry commends: "We must not be ashamed to own the secret transactions between Christ and our souls; but, when called to it, mention, to his praise, and the encouragement of others, what he has done for our souls."

The woman's full confession, made in fear and trembling, is the open avowal of what had been done secretly. Compelled by Christ's gaze, she came forward, fell down, and told the whole truth — her disease, her touch, her cure. The fear that gripped her was needless, for Christ sought not to rebuke but to bless; yet it is the common experience of the trembling believer, who fears where there is no cause.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the woman, trembling with needless fear, came and fell before Christ and confessed the whole truth — the open avowal of her secret act of faith. Her fear, though groundless, mirrors the trembling of the believer who, conscious of unworthiness, dreads where there is no cause for dread. The remedy, as Henry teaches, is to fall at Christ's feet and refer all to him. Her full confession, made to his praise and the encouragement of others, models the believer's duty not to be ashamed of the secret dealings between Christ and the soul.
