The trembling suppliant would receive, not rebuke, but the tenderest word of all: "Daughter, thy faith hath made thee whole" (v.34).
34:
"And he said unto her, Daughter, thy faith hath made thee whole; go in peace, and be whole of thy plague." The witnesses dwell on the tenderness of Christ's word and the place it gives to faith. Gill: "Instead of reproving her... he speaks to her in a very soft, kind, and tender manner, and called her daughter" — a title both of affection and of spiritual relation, for "she was a daughter of the Lord God Almighty, as her faith showed her to be." Barnes: "A word of kindness, tending to inspire confidence, and to dissipate her fears. Be whole — that is, continue to be whole; for she was already cured." Clarke: "Rather, continue whole, not, be whole, for she was already healed: but this contains a promise... that her disorder should afflict her no more."

On "thy faith hath made thee whole," the witnesses are careful. It was not the touch itself, nor any virtue in the garment, but faith laying hold of Christ's power, that healed her. Bede (under v.21): "He said not, Thy faith is about to make thee whole, but has made thee whole, that is, in that thou hast believed, thou hast already been made whole." He calls her "daughter because she was saved by her faith; for faith in Christ makes us His children." Henry (under v.21): "Christ puts honour upon faith, because faith gives honour to Christ."

On "go in peace," Ellicott notes "the true meaning of the Greek is Go into peace," and Robertson adds the Hebrew sense: "Peace here may have more the idea of the Hebrew shalom, health of body and soul." Calvin: "the benefit which she had obtained was fully ratified, when she heard from the lips of Christ what she had already learned from experience: for we do not truly... enjoy God's benefits in any other way than by possessing them as contained in the treasury of his promises."

The witnesses agree: Christ, far from rebuking the trembling woman, addressed her with the tenderest word — "Daughter" — commended her faith as the means of her healing, and dismissed her into peace, confirming by his word the cure she had already received. The word "thy faith hath made thee whole" honours faith without crediting it with any inherent power; it was faith's grasp of Christ that healed her. The cure, already wrought in her body, was now ratified by the promise of his lips, that she might possess it with a settled conscience. The secret, stolen cure becomes an open, confirmed, and blessed gift — and the once-unclean woman is sent away a daughter, whole and at peace. (The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown fragment here belongs to Matthew 27, the women at the cross.)
35:
"While he yet spake, there came from the ruler of the synagogue's house certain which said, Thy daughter is dead: why troublest thou the Master any further?" The witnesses note the crushing news that seemed to end all hope. The Antiochene and Latin fathers read both the messengers' despair and Christ's response. Theophylact: "because she had already expired, they thought that He ought not to be asked... But the Lord Himself persuades the father to have confidence." Augustine clarifies the harmony with Matthew (who has the father report her dead): the messengers' news, not Jairus's own words, announced the death. Robertson: "It was all over, so they felt. Jesus had raised from the dead the son of the widow of Nain, but people in general did not expect him to raise the dead... There was no further use in troubling the Teacher about the girl." Clarke: "These people seem to have had no other notion of our Lord than that of an eminent physician, who might be useful while there was life, but afterwards could do nothing."

The Latin fathers draw out the "sleep" that Christ would call it. Bede (anticipating v.39): "to men she was dead, who were unable to raise her up; but to God she was asleep, in whose purpose both the soul was living, and the flesh was resting, to rise again. Whence it became a custom amongst Christians, that the dead... should be said to sleep." Bede also gives the great typology: "as soon as the Church of the Gentiles is washed from the stain of vice... the synagogue is broken up on account of its zealous treachery and envy." The daughter "twelve years old" and the woman's "twelve years" mark that "the sinning of unbelievers was contemporary with the beginning of the faith of believers."

The puritan Henry (covering vv.35-43): "While there is life, there is hope... but when life is gone, it is past recall." Yet here "the death of the child doth not, as usually, put an end to the narrative." On Christ's encouragement: "Faith is the only remedy against disquieting grief and fear at such a time: let that silence them, Only believe... Believe the resurrection, and then be not afraid." Henry notes the proof the mourners gave: "Their laughing Christ to scorn... serves for the proof of this" — that the child was truly dead.

The witnesses agree: the news of the child's death seemed to end all hope, for the messengers (and the world) supposed Christ's power, like a physician's, reached only to the living. But Christ would turn the father's despair to faith, calling the death a sleep from which he would wake her. The delay caused by the woman's cure, far from being a loss, had given Jairus a fresh proof of Christ's power on which to rest his faith. The verse marks the deepening of the trial — death itself now the obstacle — and the call to a faith that believes the resurrection.
36:
"As soon as Jesus heard the word that was spoken, he saith unto the ruler of the synagogue, Be not afraid, only believe." The witnesses note Christ's prompt word of encouragement to the bereaved father. Gill: he spoke "as soon as Jesus had heard the word... that his daughter was dead, and it was to no purpose to give him any further trouble." Robertson explores the participle: "the other sense of hearing aside, overhearing what was not spoken directly to him, probably exists also. Jesus might overhear what was said and disregard its import. Certainly he ignored the conclusion of the messengers." Clarke: "These words were spoken... to prevent that distress which he otherwise must have felt on finding that the case was now, humanly speaking, hopeless."

The Reformed Calvin draws out the doctrine of faith and God's power: "The message about her death had induced despair... Christ therefore bids him take care lest, by fear or distrust, he shut out that grace, to which death will be no hindrance." The lesson is large: "God would be much more liberal in his communications to us, if we were not so close; but our own scanty desires hinder him from pouring out his gifts upon us in greater abundance... we cannot go beyond bounds in believing: because our faith, however large, will never embrace the hundredth part of the divine goodness."

Henry (under v.35) had drawn the same: "Be not afraid, only believe. Keep up a confidence in Christ, and a dependence upon him, and he will do what is for the best. Believe the resurrection, and then be not afraid." The word bids the father not to let the news of death extinguish his faith, for death is no hindrance to Christ.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ, overhearing the message of death and ignoring its despairing conclusion, at once steadied the father with the word "Be not afraid, only believe." The encouragement bids Jairus not to shut out grace by fear or distrust, for death is no obstacle to the power of Christ. Calvin's lesson is the heart of it: our faith can never overreach God's power or goodness; it is our narrow desires, not his limited grace, that straiten his gifts to us. The father, whose faith had asked Christ to heal a dying child, is now called to a larger faith that believes Christ can raise a dead one. The word "only believe" sustains him through the interval until, at the child's bedside, that faith is rewarded with her resurrection (vv.41-42).
37:
"And he suffered no man to follow him, save Peter, and James, and John the brother of James." The witnesses note Christ's selection of the inner three and the reasons for excluding the crowd. Gill: "he dismissed the multitude, being not desirous of the honour and applause of men." Robertson: "Probably the house was too small for the other disciples to come in with the family. The first instance of this inner circle of three seen again on the Mount of Transfiguration and in the Garden of Gethsemane. The one article in the Greek treats the group as a unit."

The Reformed Calvin gives two reasons for limiting the witnesses: "either because they were unworthy to be his witnesses of the miracle, or because he did not choose that the miracle should be overpowered by a noisy crowd around him. It was better that the young woman, whose dead body they had beheld, should suddenly go out before the eyes of men, alive and full of vigour." Chrysostom (under v.35) had given the same: "to take away all display, He suffered not all to be with Him; that, however, He might leave behind Him witnesses of His divine power, He chose His three chief disciples."

Henry (under v.35) noted the balance: "a competent number to be witnesses of the miracle, but not such a number as that his taking them with him might look like vainglory." Wesley adds a historical note on the naming: "John, the brother of James — when St. Mark wrote, not long after our Lord's ascension, the memory of St. James, lately beheaded, was so fresh, that his name was more known than that of John himself."

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which records the selection of the three. The witnesses agree: Christ took only Peter, James, and John as witnesses of the raising — enough to attest the miracle, but not so many as to savour of ostentation or to crowd the chamber of death. The exclusion of the multitude guarded against display and kept the solemn act from being overpowered by a noisy throng. The three form the inner circle present also at the Transfiguration and in Gethsemane — chosen witnesses of Christ's glory and his agony, and here of his power over death. The verse marks the quiet, private setting of the miracle, in keeping with Christ's repeated refusal of vainglory; the raising would be done before few witnesses, and even they charged to silence (v.43), reserving the full display of his power over death for his own resurrection.
38:
"And he cometh to the house of the ruler of the synagogue, and seeth the tumult, and them that wept and wailed greatly." The witnesses note the scene of noisy mourning that met Christ at the house. Barnes: "The tumult — the confusion and weeping of the assembled people. Wailed — making inarticulate, mournful sounds, howling for the dead." Ellicott explains the word: it "is the same as that in 1 Corinthians 13:1, in connection with the tinkling (or better, clanging) sound of a cymbal, and, formed as it is from an interjection, alala, is applied to the inarticulate cries either of despair or victory." Robertson: "An onomatopoetic word from Pindar down. The soldiers on entering battle cried Alala. Used of clanging cymbals... It is used here of the monotonous wail of the hired mourners."

The tumult, the witnesses note, was the customary noisy mourning of the East, including (per Matthew 9:23) the flute-players and the hired wailing women. Henry (under v.35) marked that "the child was extremely well beloved, for the relations and neighbours wept and wailed greatly." Yet much of the noise was professional and ostentatious — the hired mourners making bedlam, as Robertson puts it, "as if that showed grief with their ostentatious noise."

The scene of clamorous grief stands in contrast to the calm authority of Christ, who would still the tumult with a word (v.39) and turn the mourning into amazement (v.42). The wailing, however sincere in the family, was for the most part the formal, hired lamentation that attended every death — and it bore unwitting testimony to the reality of the child's death, for the mourners would not have been summoned for one merely sleeping.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which sets the scene of mourning. The witnesses agree: Christ came to the house and found the tumult of noisy grief — the wailing relatives and the hired mourners with their clanging, inarticulate cries. The clamour, customary in Eastern mourning, both expressed the family's real sorrow and, in the hired wailers, displayed the ostentatious noise that attended every death. The din stands in pointed contrast to Christ's calm — he who would silence it with the word "she is not dead, but sleepeth" (v.39). The mourning, with its flute-players and professional wailers, bore witness that the child was truly dead; and into that house of clamorous grief Christ enters as the Lord of life, to turn the wailing into a great astonishment (v.42). The verse prepares for the contrast between the world's despairing tumult and the quiet power of the Author of life.
39:
"And when he was come in, he saith unto them, Why make ye this ado, and weep? the damsel is not dead, but sleepeth." The witnesses note Christ's startling word and its meaning. Gill: he came "into the house... where the company of mourners, and the pipers, and mourning women were." Robertson: "Jesus had dismissed one crowd, but finds the house occupied by the hired mourners making bedlam... Jesus undoubtedly meant that she was not dead to stay dead, though some hold that the child was not really dead. It is a beautiful word (she is sleeping) that Jesus uses of death." Barnes cross-references John 11:11-13, where Christ calls Lazarus's death a sleep.

On the saying "not dead, but sleepeth," the fathers (under v.35) gave the true sense. Pseudo-Jerome: "Both are true, for the meaning is, She is dead to you, but to Me she is asleep." Bede: "to men she was dead, who were unable to raise her up; but to God she was asleep, in whose purpose both the soul was living, and the flesh was resting, to rise again." The child was truly dead, as the mourners' scorn (v.40) confirms; but to Christ, who would raise her, death was no more than a sleep from which he would wake her.

The Reformed Calvin: "Christ expressly makes a distinction between death and sleep... so as to excite an expectation of life. His meaning is, You will presently see her raised up whom you suppose to be dead. That he was laughed to scorn by thoughtless and ignorant people... ought not to awaken surprise. And yet this very circumstance was an additional confirmation of the miracle, that those persons entertained no doubt whatever as to her death."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ stilled the tumult and called the dead child asleep — not denying her death, but declaring, as the Lord of life, that to him her death was but a sleep from which he would wake her. The saying both comforts and challenges: she is dead to the helpless mourners, asleep to the One who can raise her. The scorn it provoked (v.40) confirms that she was truly dead, and so magnifies the miracle. From Christ's word arose, the fathers note, the Christian custom of calling the dead "asleep" — for to those who die in him, death is but a sleep before the resurrection. The verse sets the calm certainty of Christ against the despairing clamour of the world, and prepares for the waking word, "Talitha cumi" (v.41).
40:
"And they laughed him to scorn. But when he had put them all out, he taketh the father and the mother of the damsel, and them that were with him, and entereth in where the damsel was lying." The witnesses note the mourners' scornful laughter and Christ's authoritative clearing of the house. Robertson: "They jeered at him. Note imperfect tense. They kept it up... The loud laughter was ill suited to the solemn occasion. But Jesus on his part took charge of the situation... Having put out the rest by a stern assertion of authority as if he were master of the house, Jesus takes along with him these five and enters the chamber of death." He adds: "He had to use pressure to make the hired mourners leave. The presence of some people will ruin the atmosphere for spiritual work." Ellicott: "Here again the verb implies continuous action."

The scornful laughter, the witnesses note, both proved the child truly dead and showed the mourners unworthy to witness the miracle. Bede (under v.35): "Because they chose rather to laugh at than to believe in this saying concerning her resurrection, they are deservedly excluded from the place, as unworthy to witness His power in raising her." Henry (under v.35): "Christ put those out as unworthy to be witnesses of the miracle, who were noisy in their sorrow, and were so ignorant in the things of God, as not to understand him when he spoke of death as a sleep, or so scornful as to ridicule him for it."

Clarke notes the witnesses Christ retained: "The father and the mother — prudence required that they should be present, and be witnesses of the miracle. And them that were with him — that is, Peter, James, and John." Bede (under v.35) drew the mystical lesson: "if a crowd of worldly thoughts be not first cast out from the hidden parts of the heart, the soul, which lies dead within, cannot rise."

The witnesses agree: the mourners' scornful laughter, which proved the child's real death and revealed their unbelief, led Christ to put them all out and admit only the parents and the three disciples to witness the raising. The clearing of the scoffing crowd, by a stern assertion of authority, both befitted the solemnity of the act and excluded the unworthy from beholding it. The mystical lesson (Bede) is that the soul cannot rise until the crowd of worldly thoughts is cast out. Into the cleared chamber of death, with the five chosen witnesses, Christ enters to wake the sleeping child — the scornful shut out, the believing admitted, the Author of life alone able to do what all the mourners could not. (The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown fragment here belongs to Matthew 27, the sealed tomb.)
41:
"And he took the damsel by the hand, and said unto her, Talitha cumi; which is, being interpreted, Damsel, I say unto thee, arise." The witnesses treasure the Aramaic words preserved by Mark and note the quickening power of Christ's voice and touch. Ellicott: "Here, as in the Ephphatha of Mark 7:34, the Evangelist gives the very syllables which had fallen from the lips of the Healer, and been proved to be words of power... the stress laid on the Aramaic words in these instances, as in the Eli, Eli, lama sabachthani on the cross, shows that they attracted a special notice." Robertson: "These precious Aramaic words, spoken by Jesus to the child, Peter heard and remembered so that Mark gives them to us... Damsel, arise. All three Gospels mention the fact that Jesus took her by the hand, a touch of life, giving confidence and help."

On the added "I say unto thee," Jerome (under v.35) had defended the Evangelist against the charge of expansion: the words express that Christ's "meaning was to call and command her." Lightfoot draws the contrast with Jewish usage: when physicians gave physic they said "Arise from thy flux," meaning "I wish thou wouldst arise"; "but Christ saith, Maid, arise; that is, I command thee, arise." Henry (under v.35) drew it out: "the dead have not power to arise, therefore power goes along with this word, to make it effectual... Christ works while he commands, and works by the command."

The Reformed Calvin: "Christ intended to give a magnificent display of the power of his voice... the great efficacy of the voice of Christ, which reaches even to the dead, and exerts a quickening influence on death itself." Chrysostom (under v.35): "the hand of Jesus, having a quickening power, quickens the dead body, and His voice raises her as she is lying."

The witnesses agree: Christ took the dead child by the hand and woke her with a word — "Talitha cumi" — the very Aramaic syllables Peter remembered and Mark preserved. The touch and the voice together display the quickening power of Christ over death: his is no physician's wish but a sovereign command, which carries power to accomplish what it bids. Henry's image holds: "Christ works while he commands, and works by the command." The word that wakes the dead child is the same gospel word that wakes the soul dead in sin — "Awake, and arise from the dead" (Ephesians 5:14) — effectual not by the hearer's power but by the power that goes with the command of the Lord of life.
42:
"And straightway the damsel arose, and walked; for she was of the age of twelve years. And they were astonished with a great astonishment." The witnesses note the instant, complete restoration and the amazement it produced. Gill: "As soon as ever the above words were pronounced by Christ, such divine power went along with them, that the child returned to life; and raised herself up from the bed, got off of it, and walked about the room in perfect health and strength." Robertson: "Aorist tense (single act) followed by the imperfect (the walking went on). For she was twelve years old. The age mentioned by Mark alone and here as explanation that she was old enough to walk." Ellicott notes the placement of the age: "St. Mark gives the age at the end of the narrative, St. Luke at the beginning, St. Matthew not at all; a proof of a certain measure of independence in dealing with the materials."

The walking, the witnesses note, proved the cure complete. The Gloss (under v.35): "By her walking, she is shewn to have been not only raised up, but also perfectly cured." She was old enough to walk (twelve years), and she rose and walked — not weak and convalescent, but in full health and strength. Henry (under v.35) drew the spiritual lesson: "Spiritual life will appear by our rising from the bed of sloth and carelessness, and our walking in a religious conversation."

The amazement — "astonished with a great astonishment" — the witnesses note as the natural response to so great a miracle. Robertson: "with a great ecstasy, especially on the part of the parents, and no wonder." Henry (under v.35) marked its limit: "Their wonder should have worked forward to a lively faith, but it rested in a stupor or astonishment."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: at Christ's word the dead child instantly rose and walked, in full health and strength, and all were overwhelmed with astonishment. The walking proved the raising complete — not a mere flicker of life but a perfect restoration. The detail that she was twelve, given by Mark at the narrative's close, explains that she was old enough to walk, and bears the mark of independent eyewitness testimony. The amazement of the parents and disciples was the fitting response to so stupendous an act; yet, as Henry warns, wonder should ripen into faith, not rest in mere astonishment. The raised and walking child is a figure of the soul raised to spiritual life, which shows its new life by rising from sloth and walking in newness of conversation. The verse displays the completeness of Christ's power over death — instant, total, and beyond all doubt.
43:
"And he charged them straitly that no man should know it; and commanded that something should be given her to eat." The witnesses note the charge to secrecy and the tender provision of food. On the silence, Gill: Christ gave the charge "to show his dislike of ostentation and popular applause, and to avoid the envy of the Scribes and Pharisees... his time not being yet come to die." Henry (under v.35) added: "his own resurrection was to be the great instance of his power over death, and therefore the divulging of other instances must be reserved till that great proof was given."

On the command to give her food, the witnesses dwell most. Barnes: "He had raised her by extraordinary power, but he willed that she should be sustained by ordinary means. He also in this gave full evidence that she was really restored to life and health." Clarke draws out the principle: "though he had employed an extraordinary power to bring her to life, he wills that she should be continued in existence by the use of ordinary means... When the miraculous power of God is necessary, let it be resorted to: when it is not necessary, let the ordinary means be used. To act otherwise would be to tempt God." Lightfoot adds the rabbinic parallel and notes the food proved her "in a most perfect state of health, and hungry."

The provision of food, the witnesses agree, served two ends: to prove the reality and completeness of the cure (a ghost or a still-dying child would not eat), and to teach that the miraculous and the ordinary are not opposed — Christ, having raised her by his power, would have her sustained by bread. Henry (under v.35) drew the comfort: "where Christ hath given spiritual life, he will provide food for the support and nourishment of it unto life eternal."

The witnesses agree: Christ charged the witnesses to silence — reserving the full display of his power over death for his own resurrection, and avoiding both ostentation and the envy of his enemies — and tenderly commanded that the raised child be given food, proving her cure complete and teaching that extraordinary power does not dispense with ordinary means. The eating both confirmed the miracle (she was truly alive and well) and modelled the wisdom that uses natural means where the miraculous is not needed, lest we tempt God. The chapter closes with the same tender care that marked the whole: the Lord who raised the dead child stoops to see her fed. Calvin (under v.34) had noted that Christ "freely used outward signs, when the time required them"; here he joins the extraordinary act of raising to the ordinary provision of bread, and so crowns the miracle of life with the gentle care that sustains it.
